title: Merluza en salsa verde
subtitle: Basque cod in green parsley and white wine sauce
excerpt: Thick cod fillets (or hake) cooked gently in a green sauce of garlic, olive oil, white wine, fish stock, and a large amount of flat parsley, with clams added to open in the same pan. A Basque classic in which the sauce is built by the constant swirling motion of the pan, an ancient technique that emulsifies the olive oil into the stock.
sourceNotes: Merluza en salsa verde is one of the most important dishes of the Basque Country and broader northern Spain. It appears in cookbooks from the nineteenth century and is considered a foundation of cocina vasca. The salsa verde technique (where olive oil, garlic, white wine, stock, and parsley are emulsified by swirling the pan) is the defining technique of Basque fish cookery. The dish is traditionally made with hake (merluza), which is the preferred fish of Basque cooking, but works equally well with cod.

content.text: The salsa verde is built by emulsification, not by thickening with flour. Olive oil and garlic go into the pan first, the wine and stock are added and reduced, and then the pan is swirled (a constant circular motion) while the fish cooks. The gelatin released from the fish skin and the starch from the parsley both help bind the sauce, but it is the swirling that holds it together. Stop swirling and add too much heat and the sauce breaks.

The fish must be thick-cut and skin-on. Thin fillets break apart during the swirling. Start the fish skin-side down over medium heat, the skin crisps in the oil before the liquid goes in, and the skin's gelatin dissolves into the sauce as it finishes cooking. The clams open in the sauce in the last few minutes and release their brine, which seasons the whole dish.

items.name: Clams
items.prepNote: soaked in salted cold water for 30 minutes, drained

content.text: Start the sauce and sear the fish

Season the {{cod-fillet}} portions. Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} and cook gently for 2 minutes until softened but not coloured. Add the fish skin-side down. Cook for 3 minutes until the skin begins to set. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} and let it bubble for 1 minute. Add the {{stock-fish}} and half the {{parsley-flat}}.

Reduce the heat to medium-low. Using a circular motion, swirl the pan constantly for 5 to 6 minutes while the fish cooks, this movement emulsifies the oil into the stock and builds the sauce. The sauce should become slightly creamy and opaque. Add the {{clams}}, cover, and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until all the clams have opened. Discard any that remain closed. Turn the fish once if the skin side is deeply golden. Scatter the remaining {{parsley-flat}} over and serve immediately with crusty bread to mop the sauce.

items.fix: Keep the heat at medium-low and swirl constantly; if it breaks, remove from heat and add a spoonful of cold water while swirling vigorously
items.cause: Heat too high, or pan stopped moving
items.symptom: Sauce has broken and is oily

items.fix: Use thick-cut fillets; the swirling motion moves the liquid around the fish, not the fish itself, do not flip repeatedly
items.cause: Fillets too thin or moved with a spatula rather than swirled
items.symptom: Fish has fallen apart during cooking

items.fix: Increase to medium heat with the lid on; clams that are still closed after a further 2 minutes are dead, discard them
items.cause: Not enough heat under the covered pan
items.symptom: Clams have not opened after 4 minutes

items.fix: Remove the fish and reduce the sauce over higher heat for 2 minutes before returning the fish; the sauce should be lightly syrupy
items.cause: Too much liquid for the amount of fish
items.symptom: Sauce is too thin and watery

content.text: Merluza en salsa verde is the flagship dish of Basque cooking. The ingredients are simple. The technique is demanding. The dish is fully its own thing. The Basque Country has the highest count of Michelin-starred restaurants in the world. Its food culture prizes technique over fuss. This dish shows both. Six ingredients, one pan, and the swirl of the pan as the cooking move. Hake was the common fish of the Basque fishing fleets in the Bay of Biscay and the Atlantic. The clams came from the river estuaries. You cook this dish when the fish is very fresh and you want nothing to get in the way.
